entities.description: A public keyed representation of Campbell's section VI: two witness-labeled verse streams, retained Latin rubrics, editorial notes, printed-page breaks, manuscript-folio milestones, and typography. It excludes the article's pp. 529–544 introduction and pp. 611–613 final notes.

rights.basis: The U-M collection labels the material 'No Copyright–United States.' This is jurisdiction-specific and does not control manuscript photographs.

entities.description: An anonymous late-thirteenth-century Middle English metrical life of Christ based ultimately on the Gospels and probably mediated in part through Peter Comestor's Historia scholastica or a related compilation. Seven manuscript versions survive in fragmentary or revised form.
entities.label: La Estorie del Evangelie

rights.basis: The work was composed in the late thirteenth century and its author is anonymous. This does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, photographs, or provider interfaces.
rights.component: Underlying medieval text
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions applying ordinary copyright terms

bibliographic.citation: Gertrude H. Campbell, 'The Middle English Evangelie,' Publications of the Modern Language Association of America 30, no. 3 (1915): 529–613, https://doi.org/10.1632/456948.
bibliographic.container_title: Publications of the Modern Language Association of America
bibliographic.title: The Middle English Evangelie

entities.description: The immediate printed source of CME00079. Campbell's article describes the manuscripts and textual relations, studies sources, dialect, and meter, and in section VI prints the Dulwich fragment and Bodleian version. CME encodes only section VI, pp. 545–610.
entities.label: Gertrude H. Campbell, The Middle English Evangelie (1915)

physicalDescription.extent: Article pp. 529–613; encoded section VI pp. 545–610
physicalDescription.format: Journal article with parallel single-witness diplomatic texts
notes: The Dulwich and Bodleian texts occupy parallel printed zones until the Dulwich fragment ends; the Bodleian version then continues alone.

rights.basis: The article was published in the United States in 1915. This statement does not grant rights in Cambridge Core interface assets or later metadata.
rights.component: 1915 article, transcription, and apparatus

access.entityLabel: London, Dulwich College, MS 22
notes: No complete public image object for MS 22 was found in the current Dulwich archive pages, the Henslowe-Alleyn digitization catalog, or exact-shelfmark searches. The Henslowe-Alleyn project's public images concern selected theatrical manuscripts and do not establish digitization of MS 22.
This dated negative finding does not prove that the manuscript has never been photographed or that internal images do not exist.

access.entityLabel: London, Dulwich College, MS 22
notes: The archive welcomes enquiries and research appointments; in-depth research may incur a fee. Enquiries may be sent to archives@dulwich.org.uk.

Request a complete-object surrogate, not a work excerpt: front and rear covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves and blanks, every recto and verso of leaves 1–89, and edges when offered. State separately that the Evangelie is currently cited at fols. 81v–85v and ask the archivist to confirm the foliation conflict.
Ask for an item-specific quote, image specifications, delivery terms, and separate permission terms for repository redistribution or commercial publication.

rights.basis: The archive invites enquiries, while its reproduction page directs requests for manuscript photographs and permission to the archive. No MS-22-specific delivery or reuse terms have been obtained.
rights.component: Any manuscript photographs supplied by Dulwich College
rights.jurisdiction: Dulwich College delivery and permission terms

dateStatements.label: Composite codex
dateStatements.value: Thirteenth and fourteenth centuries, with some later additions

entities.description: A composite vellum theological miscellany of 89 leaves in different thirteenth- and fourteenth-century hands. The Evangelie fragment is an inserted quire copied around 1300 and now localized to South Lincolnshire.
entities.label: London, Dulwich College, MS 22

notes: The work is currently cited at fols. 81v–85v; Campbell's introduction and Warner's catalog give a shorter terminal locator, while Campbell's printed milestones reach fol. 85v.
The inserted quire originally belonged to another manuscript; its two outer leaves are lost. The first three surviving pages contain Latin theological material before the poem.
The poem is written in double columns, approximately 30–41 lines per column, with rubricated Latin passages and colored line initials.
Adjacent item 10 is a French Miracles of the Virgin fragment; it belongs to the complete physical object but not to this work.

rights.basis: The archive invites enquiries, while its reproduction page directs requests for manuscript photographs and permission to the archive. No MS-22-specific delivery or reuse terms have been obtained.
rights.component: Any manuscript photographs supplied by Dulwich College
rights.jurisdiction: Dulwich College delivery and permission terms

access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Add. C. 38
access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans supplied by file transfer

localCopies.label: Bodleian mediated copying order form

notes: The service is available to all, ordinarily charges non-Oxford requesters £1 per scan with a £10 minimum, and assesses conservation and legal suitability case by case. Completed forms and questions may be emailed to mediated.copying@bodleian.ox.ac.uk.

For complete-source coverage request the entire physical object: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, the front flyleaf, all rectos and versos of manuscript leaves 1–121 including blanks, and edges when offered. State separately that the Evangelie occupies fols. 71v–82r (Campbell 71b–82a).
Reference scans are not automatically authorized for repository redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use.

rights.basis: The mediated-copying form states that scans of out-of-copyright material are © Bodleian Libraries and supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0; the fuller terms prohibit commercial reuse without permission. No item-specific delivery terms have yet been received.
rights.component: Future reference-quality scans supplied through Bodleian mediated copying

access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Add. C. 38
notes: Advance ordering, reader registration, and conservation restrictions apply.

entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century English parchment codex containing part of the South English Legendary and La Estorie del Euangelie. Campbell's transcription places the Evangelie at fols. 71v–82r under a marginal Latin title.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Add. C. 38

physicalDescription.extent: i + 121 leaves according to the Summary Catalogue description quoted by Campbell
physicalDescription.format: Codex containing part of the South English Legendary and the Evangelie
notes: The work occupies fols. 71v–82r in current recto/verso notation; Campbell prints the legacy form 71b–82a.
Campbell describes one column with 40–46 written lines per page, normally placing both verses of an original couplet on one manuscript line.
The current catalog dates the codex 1400–1410; Campbell quotes the older 1410–1420 assessment.
The physical codex is described as imperfect at the end, which is distinct from whether its Evangelie version reaches a deliberate textual ending.

rights.basis: The mediated-copying form states that scans of out-of-copyright material are © Bodleian Libraries and supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0; the fuller terms prohibit commercial reuse without permission. No item-specific delivery terms have yet been received.

entities.description: A large, richly illuminated late-fourteenth-century West Midlands religious miscellany. Its 396-line Evangelie fragment occupies fols. 105r–105v and supplies the Anglo-Norman heading from which the conventional work title derives.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves; originally viii + 413, with 71 leaves thought lost
notes: The Evangelie occupies fols. 105r–105v, Digital Bodleian canvases 193–194, and is incomplete after 396 lines.
Seven miniatures survive within this two-page fragment.

access.entityLabel: London, Senate House Library, MS SLV/17
notes: The Special Collections Reading Room provides onsite access. A free Virtual Reading Room service is described for members and external researchers; contact the library to arrange either route.

entities.description: A manuscript dated 1403 whose present 114 leaves comprise 97 leaves of Piers Plowman followed by a 17-leaf La Estorie del Evangelie witness at ff. 97v–111v. Pons-Sanz reconstructs 1,764 Estorie lines and localizes its copy dialect to central Worcestershire.
entities.label: London, Senate House Library, MS SLV/17

notes: Piers Plowman occupies 97 leaves and La Estorie del Evangelie 17 leaves; the work locus is ff. 97v–111v.

Compare a complete supplied surrogate against Campbell's folio milestones and incipit/explicit.
Retain the provisional modern scholarly range rather than silently correcting the historical description.
openQuestions.question: What is the archive's current authoritative foliation for the Dulwich fragment: fols. 81v–85v or the shorter range reported in Campbell's introductory catalog quotation?

next_steps: Search Campbell and Carleton Brown papers for correspondence, notes, or photographs.
Ask the Bodleian whether early-twentieth-century reproduction or reader records survive.
Do not invent a photographic intermediary for B without evidence.
openQuestions.question: Did Campbell transcribe MS. Add. C. 38 directly from the codex, from photographs, or through another working copy?

next_steps: Consult Millward's 1998 parallel-text edition directly, especially its reconstructed lineation and ending analysis.
Distinguish a coherent witness ending from completeness relative to the lost archetype.
Keep Campbell's and modern claims separately attributed.
openQuestions.question: How should Campbell's description of B as a complete text be reconciled with modern scholarship that treats all seven versions as incomplete relative to the reconstructed tradition?

next_steps: Review Millward's source study and later biblical-literature scholarship.
Keep Historia scholastica as a probable related source, not an identified physical exemplar.
Separate sources for individual bestiary, homiletic, and apocryphal passages from the main narrative source.
openQuestions.question: What compilation, if any, mediated between the Gospels, Peter Comestor and the Estorie author?

next_steps: Request full-object quotes using the distinct surface inventories recorded in each access record; do not reduce requests to the target-work folios.
Ask each repository to confirm existing photography or microfilm before commissioning new work.
Obtain written, item-specific redistribution and commercial-use terms before placing delivered images in Git or publication packages.
Recheck Senate House reproduction availability after September 2026.
openQuestions.question: Can the holding institutions supply complete color surrogates of Dulwich MS 22, Bodleian MSS Add. C. 38 and Rawl. C. 655, Senate House SLV/17, Royal MS 17 C XVII, and Lansdowne MS 388 with terms permitting local study or broader reuse?

next_steps: Review Millward 1998 and McIntosh 1987 directly.
Keep the copy dialects of D and B separate from the reconstructed original dialect.
Avoid converting dialect geography into a certain place of composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the late-thirteenth-century poem be localized more narrowly than the south Lincolnshire / north-west Norfolk / north-east Cambridgeshire Fenland zone?

next_steps: Concord Foster's Northern Passion lineation and the current catalog's interpolation runs to Rawl. C. 655 folios.
Do not label the one-canvas Digital Bodleian object as target-work coverage until the concordance proves overlap.
Request the complete codex rather than only the published opening.
openQuestions.question: Which Rawl. C. 655 folios contain each of the three Estorie interpolations, and does the public ff. 17v–18r opening overlap any of them?

next_steps: Inspect the authenticated item inventory through a legitimate subscription or ask Bloomsbury and Senate House Library for exact coverage.
Do not treat the subscription object as an Estorie facsimile until ff. 97v–111v are confirmed.
Use the Virtual Reading Room to verify target folios while reproduction service is suspended.
openQuestions.question: Does Bloomsbury item BMS_SHL003 include the 17-leaf Estorie portion of SLV/17, or only the provider-described 97-leaf Piers Plowman portion?

next_steps: Inspect the complete codex or an authoritative item-level description to resolve the f. 373r/f. 376 boundary.
Ask Folger or Adam Matthew for the reel-8 frame inventory before asserting target or whole-codex coverage.
Retain both claims as attributed evidence rather than silently choosing one.
openQuestions.question: How should Pons-Sanz's Lansdowne Estorie locus ff. 373r–380v be reconciled with the current item record beginning at f. 376, and does Folger reel 8 cover those folios or the whole manuscript?

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual unit is one anonymous Middle English metrical Gospel poem represented by two separately serialized witness versions, not two independent works.

Campbell calls MS. Add. C. 38 a complete text, but current scholarship reconstructs a longer tradition from seven mutually non-derivative and individually incomplete witnesses. The Bodleian version is therefore described as Campbell's longest printed version rather than a complete authorial text.

Campbell's introductory Dulwich locus ends at fol. 84v, while her own transcription milestones reach fol. 85v and current scholarship locates the fragment at fols. 81v–85v. The modern range is retained provisionally and the discrepancy remains open.

The complete-source rule applies independently to each physical witness. Requests cover the whole surviving physical object and binding surfaces for Dulwich, Add. C. 38, Rawl. C. 655, SLV/17, Royal 17 C XVII, and Lansdowne 388, with work loci stated separately rather than used as crop boundaries.
The Vernon Manuscript is collateral evidence for the work title and tradition. It did not supply the two continuous texts encoded in CME00079.

lineage.summary: CME00079 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan encoding of section VI, pp. 545–610, of Gertrude H. Campbell's 1915 article 'The Middle English Evangelie.' Campbell prints two witness versions of the anonymous late-thirteenth-century Gospel poem conventionally called La Estorie del Evangelie: the 519-line opening fragment in Dulwich College MS 22 and the much longer version in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Add. C. 38. Campbell worked from Carleton Brown's rotographs for the Dulwich text; her precise working medium for the Bodleian text is unstated. The XML serializes Campbell's parallel printed witness columns into two consecutive DIV2 streams and omits the article's linguistic/source study and final notes. Pons-Sanz's modern seven-witness census adds the Vernon Manuscript and four collateral witnesses: Rawl. C. 655, Senate House SLV/17, Royal MS 17 C XVII, and Lansdowne MS 388. A complete 710-canvas Vernon facsimile is cached and mapped to ff. 105r–105v; Rawl. C. 655 has only a cached one-opening public object whose target overlap is unproven; no complete public manuscript facsimiles were found for the other six witnesses. Whole-object request scopes and the best available scholarly text reproductions are recorded separately for every witness.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Late thirteenth century; earliest surviving witness circa 1300; source edition published 1915
catalogSummary.region: Fenland zone of south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, or north-east Cambridgeshire; exact composition place uncertain
catalogSummary.title: La Estorie del Evangelie

catalogingSubject.label: La Estorie del Evangelie
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-thirteenth-century Middle English metrical life of Christ represented in CME00079 by Gertrude H. Campbell's 1915 transcriptions of two manuscript versions. The record describes one work, not two independent works and not an eclectic reconstruction. Its content parts identify the separately serialized Dulwich and Bodleian witness representations so dates, loci, and lineage remain scoped. The work is distinct from Dulwich College MS 22 and Bodleian MS. Add. C. 38 as complete physical objects, Carleton Brown's rotographs, Campbell's article, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML.

contentParts.label: Dulwich College MS 22 version
contentParts.summary: Campbell's transcription of the 519-line opening fragment, serialized from the left-hand parallel print stream.

contentParts.label: Bodleian MS. Add. C. 38 version
contentParts.summary: Campbell's longer Bodleian text, restored from two-verses-per-manuscript-line to couplet form and serialized after the Dulwich stream.

dateStatements.display: Probably composed in the late thirteenth century
notes: The date is reconstructed from the circa-1300 Dulwich witness and textual history; it is not a dated authorial colophon.

dateStatements.display: The earliest surviving witness, Dulwich College MS 22, was copied circa 1300
notes: This dates the surviving copy and establishes a terminus ante quem, not an exact composition year.

extent.description: Two serialized witness-version divisions contain 2,222 XML L elements in 133 LG groups: 519 Dulwich lines and 1,703 Bodleian line elements. Campbell's shared lineation reaches D 519 and B 1879, while Millward's reconstructed tradition places the same B version from the beginning through reconstructed line 2435. The encoding includes 98 PB elements over the overlapping physical printed range pp. 545–610, 433 MILESTONE elements, 86 notes, and 1,837 italic spans. These are encoding metrics, not an authorial line count or a count of distinct print pages.

formStatements.description: A metrical narrative preserved in monorhyming quatrains and rhymed couplets. Recurring Latin rubrics and quotations do not make the work mixed prose and verse.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Modern English editorial headnotes and footnotes belong to Campbell's edition layer and are not exposed as a work-language facet. The Anglo-Norman wording supplies the conventional title through the Vernon witness but is not part of the two CME text streams.

scope.description: Recurring manuscript rubrics and scriptural or patristic quotations within the Middle English narrative

notes: The two content parts are witness representations needed for scoped lineage and metrics; they are not authorial chapters or separate works.
Campbell's line numbers and the XML L count differ from Millward's reconstructed lineation because each witness omits, reorders, or revises material. All counts are labeled by layer.
The complete cached Vernon facsimile supports title and transmission research but is not the physical source of either continuous CME text.
Public or cached access never by itself authorizes redistribution. The Vernon images are CC BY-NC 4.0; any future Dulwich or Add. C. 38 delivery needs item-specific terms.

next_steps: Ask Dulwich College Archives to confirm whether fols. 81v–85v is the current range and explain Campbell's shorter introductory locator.
Compare a complete surrogate against the encoded folio milestones.
Keep the current mapping provisional until confirmed.
openQuestions.question: What is the current authoritative Dulwich folio range for the fragment?

next_steps: Consult Millward's 1998 parallel-text edition directly.
Separate a coherent witness ending from completeness relative to the lost archetype.
Retain both attributed claims rather than flattening them into one extent statement.
openQuestions.question: How should Campbell's 'complete text' description for B be reconciled with the modern reconstruction of seven individually incomplete versions?

next_steps: Review Millward's source analysis and later biblical-literature studies.
Keep Peter Comestor probable and scoped rather than naming an unsupported physical exemplar.
Distinguish sources for local passages from the main narrative source.
openQuestions.question: What compilation mediated the work's Gospel, Peter Comestor, bestiary, apocryphal, and homiletic materials?

next_steps: Review Millward 1998 and McIntosh 1987 directly.
Preserve the distinct copy dialects of D and B.
Do not convert reconstructed dialect geography into a certain composition place.
openQuestions.question: Can the original poem be localized more narrowly than the broad Fenland zone?

notes: Modern scholarship places the original in an East Midlands/Fenland zone from shared linguistic features; D itself is localized to South Lincolnshire and B to Worcestershire. Copy dialects are not transferred mechanically to composition place.
placeStatements.placeLabel: Fenland: south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, and north-east Cambridgeshire

scope.description: Reconstructed broad zone, not a certain settlement

placeStatements.placeLabel: Fenland: south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, and north-east Cambridgeshire

scope.description: Reconstructed dialect of the original behind the seven witnesses
Immediate source edition and witness transcriptions

subjects.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Parallel manuscript versions and textual transmission

scope.description: Discovery subject of the CME representation and Campbell edition

summaries.value: La Estorie del Evangelie is an anonymous late-thirteenth-century Middle English metrical life of Christ. It combines Gospel narrative from the Annunciation and Nativity through the Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, and Last Judgment with devotional exhortation, bestiary moralization, apocryphal episodes, and recurring Latin rubrics. CME00079 serializes Gertrude H. Campbell's 1915 parallel edition of two substantially different manuscript versions: an early 519-line Dulwich fragment and a longer, heavily revised Bodleian text. Campbell's modern English introduction and concluding source notes are omitted. Modern scholarship recognizes seven mutually non-derivative surviving witnesses; the other five are recorded as collateral transmission evidence rather than sources of the two encoded texts.

The Dulwich version opens with a penitential invocation and moralizations of the hart, serpent, and eagle, then narrates the annunciations and births of John the Baptist and Christ. Its surviving 519 lines end as Joseph and Mary travel from Nazareth to Bethlehem.

The Bodleian version begins with the same penitential frame in a modernized and abridged textual form, continues through Christ's birth, ministry, Passion, descent, Resurrection, Ascension, Pentecost, and the Last Judgment, and closes with a devotional vision of heavenly bliss and a Trinitarian Amen.

notes: The title is conventional for the Middle English work; it is not a collective title invented for the two CME witness streams.
titles.supplied_by: Conventional scholarly title from the Anglo-Norman heading preceding the Vernon fragment

notes: The longer heading continues by listing the Annunciation, Nativity, Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, glorification, Second Coming, Judgment, and presentation in body and soul.
titles.supplied_by: Anglo-Norman heading in the Vernon Manuscript
titles.value: I ceste liuere est escrit la estorie del Euangelie en engleis

titles.supplied_by: Marginal title in MS. Add. C. 38 reported by Campbell
titles.value: Nouum testamentum domini nostri ihesu christi
Sum wyle i was wyt sinne ibunde / Ant sinne me hauid cast to grunde

notes: This is the accidental end of the 519-line Dulwich fragment, not the work's authorial explicit.
titles.value: Ant of nazaret him bi-gan to hye / Ant to bethleem weys guye
Sum tyme i was with loue bounde / and synne me hath kest to grounde

notes: The witness reaches a devotional Amen; modern reconstruction still treats every surviving version as incomplete relative to the wider text tradition.
titles.value: that o god is and euer shal be / fadir and son and the holi gost in trinite. Amen

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Late thirteenth century; earliest surviving witness circa 1300; source edition published 1915

publication.embeddedTitle: VI. TEXTS OF THE MIDDLE ENGLISH EVANGELIE

work.regionDisplay: Fenland zone of south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, or north-east Cambridgeshire; exact composition place uncertain
